NYSE MARKET REPORT

PAST MONTH TOP PERFORMERS ON THE S&P 500; IN DESCENDING ORDER OF % PRICE RISE

+ TOP STOCK RANKED 1 CATERPILLAR (CAT: +$167.61 [+23.7%]).

Short Stock Dashboard

+ Caterpillar Inc. (CAT: $874.66) Market Cap $411 billion, Industrial machinery sector, P/E: 46.3, PV$1000 (20 yrs): $19,810

Past Month: Monthly: Caterpillar Inc. (NYSE:CAT) jumps 24%, leads Industrial Machinery sector

Caterpillar Inc. (NYSE:CAT $874.66), the NYSE's largest Industrial Machinery company by market cap, has jumped $167.61 (or 23.7%) in the month ended May 31 to close at $874.66. This gain makes the stock the highest rising one in the Industrial Machinery sector which is up 9.5%. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 618.1 points (or 9.4%) in the month, this represented a relative price increase of 14.3%.

The volume in the month was 0.7 times the average trading of 50.6 million shares per month.

Uptrend

-- Today's Volatility:

The 3% discount to 12-month high of $905.0 against the 178% premium to 12-month low of $314.70 suggests the stock continues to push for new highs.

Activities

Caterpillar Inc., headquartered in Deerfield, Illinois, is a global leader in the manufacturing and sale of construction and mining equipment, diesel and natural gas engines, industrial turbines, and locomotives. Founded in 1925, the company operates through four primary segments: Construction Industries, Resource Industries, Energy & Transportation, and Financial Products, serving diverse markets including infrastructure, energy, and transportation. Its core activities encompass designing, producing, and distributing machinery for earthmoving, material handling, and power generation, while also offering aftermarket parts, services, and financing solutions to enhance customer productivity. Among its fastest-growing segments are Energy & Transportation, driven by rising demand for power systems in renewable energy projects and data centers, and digital solutions like Cat Connect, which leverage IoT for equipment monitoring and predictive maintenance. This strategic focus on innovation and sustainability positions Caterpillar to capitalize on global infrastructure investments and environmental regulations. It is the NYSE's largest Industrial machinery company by market capitalisation.

Caterpillar Inc. (NYSE:CAT) stands as a global leader in the manufacturing of construction and mining equipment, offering a diversified range of products that cater to various industry needs. Its portfolio includes Caterpillar excavators, Cat hydraulic excavators, and Caterpillar wheel loaders, which are pivotal in Caterpillar earthmoving equipment. The company is renowned for providing Cat construction equipment such as Caterpillar bulldozers, Caterpillar motor graders, and Caterpillar backhoe loaders. Moreover, it excels in supplying Caterpillar mining trucks, Cat off-highway trucks, and Cat articulated trucks for large-scale projects. In the power systems domain, Caterpillar diesel engines, Cat generator sets, and Caterpillar power systems underscore its comprehensive offerings. Caterpillar engine parts, Cat fuel systems, and Caterpillar aftertreatment systems are essential components for long-term machinery efficiency. Additionally, the company supports the industry with Cat aftermarket parts, Caterpillar remanufactured parts, and Caterpillar undercarriage parts, offering solutions like Cat telehandlers, Cat compact track loaders, and Cat skid steer loaders. Its commitment extends to Caterpillar marine engines, Cat electric drive mining trucks, Caterpillar portable generators, Cat heavy machinery rental, and Caterpillar powertrain components, ensuring versatile applications across sectors.

+ TOP STOCK RANKED 2 MORGAN STANLEY (MS: +$24.31 [+14.9%]).

Short Stock Dashboard

+ Morgan Stanley (MS: $188.0) Market Cap $299 billion, Investment services sector, P/E: 18.2, PV$1000 (20 yrs): $6,331

Past Month: Monthly: Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) climbs 15%, leads Investment Services sector

Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS $188.0), the NYSE's largest Investment Services company by market cap, has climbed $24.31 (or 14.9%) in the month ended May 31 to close at $188.0. This gain makes the stock the highest rising one in the Investment Services sector which is up 5.3%. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 618.1 points (or 9.4%) in the month, this represented a relative price increase of 5.5%.

The volume in the month was 0.8 times the average trading of 112.7 million shares per month.

Activities

Morgan Stanley is a prominent global financial services firm headquartered in New York and listed on the New York Stock Exchange. As a leading investment bank, it provides a comprehensive range of services, including investment banking advisory for mergers and acquisitions, capital markets underwriting, institutional securities trading, and wealth management solutions. The firm's core activities focus on helping clients—such as corporations, governments, and individuals—navigate complex financial markets through strategic advice, asset management, and risk mitigation. In recent years, Morgan Stanley's wealth management segment has been one of the fastest-growing areas, fueled by rising assets under management and a strategic emphasis on high-net-worth clients and digital platforms. Additionally, its institutional securities division has expanded rapidly in equities and fixed-income trading, capitalizing on global market volatility and technological advancements. This diversified approach underscores Morgan Stanley's resilience and innovation in the financial sector. It is the NYSE's largest Investment services company by market capitalisation.

Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) is a leading financial services firm renowned for its comprehensive Morgan Stanley capital markets services and Morgan Stanley investment banking services. The firm provides Morgan Stanley wealth management services alongside Morgan Stanley asset management funds, including Morgan Stanley ETFs and mutual funds. As a pivotal player in the Morgan Stanley insurance sector research and Morgan Stanley P&C insurance coverage, the company focuses on addressing the insurance industry volatility Morgan Stanley analyses. Recently, developments in Morgan Stanley Kinsale Capital downgrade have impacted the Kinsale Capital stock outlook KNSL, with Morgan Stanley analyst Kinsale Capital observations leading to Kinsale Capital downgrade Morgan Stanley insights. The MS downgrade Kinsale Capital KNSL initiative and Morgan Stanley research report Kinsale Capital underscore Kinsale Capital KNSL stock downgrade ramifications, prompting a recalibration of the KNSL price target Morgan Stanley. Furthermore, Morgan Stanley market volatility analysis drives Morgan Stanley securities downgrades, reflected in Morgan Stanley stock NYSE:MS news. Their robust Morgan Stanley equity analysts reports support Morgan Stanley financial advisory services and Morgan Stanley risk management solutions, complemented by their Morgan Stanley trading platform for stocks, Morgan Stanley prime brokerage services, Morgan Stanley M&A advisory, and Morgan Stanley investment research reports. Through superior Morgan Stanley institutional securities offerings and expert Morgan Stanley market commentary Kinsale considerations, they effectively navigate insurance stocks downgrade analysis.

+ TOP STOCK RANKED 3 TAKE-TWO INTERACTIVE (TTWO: +$27.68 [+14.0%]).

Short Stock Dashboard

+ Take-Two Interactive Software, Inc. (TTWO: $225.18) Market Cap $42 billion, Textiles sector, PV$1000 (20 yrs): $13,123

Take-Two Interactive Software, Inc. (NASDAQ:TTWO $225.18), the NASDAQ's largest Textiles company by market cap, has climbed $27.68 (or 14.0%) in the month ended May 31 to close at $225.18. This gain makes the stock the highest rising one in the Textiles sector which is up 12.0%.

The volume in the month was 0.7 times the average trading of 39.2 million shares per month.

Activities

Take-Two Interactive Software, Inc., a leading Nasdaq-listed company, specializes in the development, publishing, and marketing of interactive entertainment products worldwide. Through its prominent subsidiaries, such as Rockstar Games and 2K, the company creates high-quality video games and related content across platforms like consoles, PCs, and mobile devices. Core activities include producing blockbuster franchises like Grand Theft Auto and NBA 2K, which drive revenue through initial sales, digital downloads, and in-game purchases. The fastest-growing segments are mobile gaming, bolstered by the 2022 acquisition of Zynga, and live services models that deliver ongoing updates and microtransactions, fostering sustained user engagement and recurring revenue. This strategic emphasis on innovative, multiplayer experiences has solidified Take-Two's position as a major force in the global gaming industry, adapting to shifting consumer preferences and technological advancements. It is the NASDAQ's largest Textiles company by market capitalisation.

Grand Theft Auto V (GTA V) continues to set impressive milestones with its record sales figures, reflecting the enduring popularity of its immersive gameplay. The GTA Online platform, regularly enhanced with updates and exciting DLC, further bolsters these achievements. Rockstar Games' Grand Theft Auto V maintains its relevance with its next-gen enhancements, PC version flexibility, and the vibrant modding community engaged in creating innovative GTA V mods. The financial success of GTA V significantly contributes to Take-Two Interactive Software, Inc. (NASDAQ:TTWO), with GTA V revenue being a notable factor for the company. Alongside GTA V, Red Dead Redemption 2 and its online counterpart, Red Dead Online, captivate gaming audiences globally. Take-Two also thrives with the NBA 2K series, including NBA 2K24, and other notable titles like Borderlands 3 and Mafia Definitive Edition. Acclaimed series such as BioShock, Civilization, and games from 2K Games and Private Division, like Kerbal Space Program 2 and The Outer Worlds, further solidify TTWO stock's standing in the gaming industry.

+ TOP STOCK RANKED 4 DELTA AIR LINES (DAL: +$2 [+3.0%]).

Short Stock Dashboard

+ Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL: $68.51) Market Cap $45 billion, Airlines sector, P/E: 8.9, PV$1000 (10 yrs): $1,798

Past Month: Monthly: Delta Air Lines, Inc. (NYSE:DAL) increases 3%, leads Airlines sector

Delta Air Lines, Inc. (NYSE:DAL $68.51), the NYSE's largest Airline company by market cap, has increased $2.0 (or 3.0%) in the month ended May 31 to close at $68.51. This gain makes the stock the highest rising one in the Airlines sector which is up 1.6%. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which rose 618.1 points (or 9.4%) in the month, this represented a relative price change of -6.4%.

The volume in the month was 0.9 times the average trading of 179.2 million shares per month.

Activities

Delta Air Lines, Inc., a major U.S.-based airline listed on the New York Stock Exchange, is a leading provider of global air transportation services. Headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia, the company operates an extensive network of scheduled passenger flights, cargo transportation, and aircraft maintenance, serving over 300 destinations across six continents with one of the world's largest fleets. Its core activities focus on reliable, efficient travel, supported by advanced technology and strategic alliances, such as those with SkyTeam partners. The fastest-growing segments include international routes, particularly in premium cabins and long-haul markets like Asia and Europe, as well as cargo services fueled by e-commerce demand. Additionally, Delta's SkyMiles loyalty program and investments in sustainable aviation fuels are driving revenue growth and operational efficiency in a competitive industry. It is the NYSE's largest Airline company by market capitalisation.

Delta Air Lines, Inc. (NYSE:DAL) operates an extensive SkyMiles program, which is a cornerstone of its strategic initiative to maximize Delta ancillary revenue and enhance loyalty program revenue (Delta). Through Delta SkyMiles membership, frequent flyers enjoy SkyMiles miles accrual and Medallion benefits, achieving Delta SkyMiles Medallion Status for exclusive perks. The Delta SkyMiles American Express and Delta SkyMiles credit card foster customer engagement while offering invaluable Delta co-branded card benefits. Delta's frequent flyer program facilitates seamless SkyMiles award travel and SkyMiles redemption, allowing travelers to upgrade with miles. The Delta Sky Club experience, partnered with Delta One, adds to the value proposition. Delta utilizes SkyMiles Marketplace, Delta partner airlines, and the SkyTeam alliance to expand travel flexibility and access SkyMiles transfer partners. The company's SkyMiles no expiration policy adds retention appeal. In Delta corporate travel, the Companion certificate (Delta) and SkyMiles status challenge bolster organizational relations and Upgrade clearance (Delta). This comprehensive approach contributes positively to DAL stock attractiveness and Loyalty program valuation (Delta).

urn:contentItem:6JGK-75M3-RYXJ-Y1HR-00000-00Quarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Caterpillar EPS increases 7% Corporate Wire Date: 01 May 2026 12:35 EDT Release Date: April 30, 20262026-05-02T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisQuarterly Report Analysis Quarter ending 31 March 2026: Caterpillar EPS increases 7% Corporate Wire Date: 01 May 2026 12:35 EDT Release Date: April 30, 2026

AMERICAN RESULTS

Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT), announced EPS of $5.5 for the quarter-ended 31 March 2026 [Q1/2026], up 6.8% sequentially from $5.15 in the previous quarter [Q4/2025].

Major changes compared with previous corresponding period [Mar 31, 2026 vs Mar 31, 2025]:

Favourable Changes:

- Net profit was up 27.2% from $2.0b to $2.5b

- Total revenue was up 22.2% from $14.2b to $17.4b

- EPS growth 30.3% from $4.22 to $5.5

- EBIT to Sales was up from 18% to 18.4%

- Profit before tax to Sales was up from 18% to 18.4%

- Profit after tax to Sales was up from 14.1% to 14.6%

Note:

- Tax expenses to Sales was down from 4% to 3.8%

Quarterly Report (Quarter ending 31 Mar 2026)

Quarter-ended31 Mar 202631 Dec 202530 Sep 2025EPS, $5.55.24.9Sequential growth in EPS %6.84.95.8Revenue, $ billion17.419.117.6Sequential growth in Revenue %-9.08.56.5Net Profit, $ billion2.52.42.3Sequential growth in Net Profit %6.14.45.6

Compared with the Previous Corresponding Period [PCP; Q1/2025], year-over-year [y.o.y.] EPS was up 30.3%, Revenue was up 22.2% and Net Profit was up 27.2%.

Quarter-ended31 Mar 202631 Mar 2025EPS, $5.54.2PCP growth in EPS %30-27Revenue, $ billion17.414.2PCP growth in Revenue %22.2-9.8Net Profit, $ billion2.52.0PCP growth in Net Profit %27.2-29.9

ACTIVITIES:

Caterpillar Inc., headquartered in Deerfield, Illinois, is a global leader in the manufacturing and sale of construction and mining equipment, diesel and natural gas engines, industrial turbines, and locomotives. Founded in 1925, the company operates through four primary segments: Construction Industries, Resource Industries, Energy & Transportation, and Financial Products, serving diverse markets including infrastructure, energy, and transportation. Its core activities encompass designing, producing, and distributing machinery for earthmoving, material handling, and power generation, while also offering aftermarket parts, services, and financing solutions to enhance customer productivity. Among its fastest-growing segments are Energy & Transportation, driven by rising demand for power systems in renewable energy projects and data centers, and digital solutions like Cat Connect, which leverage IoT for equipment monitoring and predictive maintenance. This strategic focus on innovation and sustainability positions Caterpillar to capitalize on global infrastructure investments and environmental regulations.

MARKET POSITION:

Caterpillar Inc. (NYSE:CAT), the NYSE's largest Industrial Machinery company by market cap, soared $320.17 (or 56.1%) year-to-date (YTD) in 2026 to close at $890.62. Compared with the S&P 500 Index which has risen 5.3% YTD, this is a relative price increase of 50.8%. A zero-bagger in 2025, the value of USD1,000 invested two years ago is $2,688 [vs $1,432 for the S&P 500 Index], for a capital gain of $1,615 and dividend reinvested of $72. The average annualised return to shareholders has been 63.9% in the past two years. The average daily volume of 1.8 million shares so far in 2026 was 0.7 times the average daily volume in 2025.

AMERICAN RESULTS

Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT), announced net profit of $2.5b for the quarter-ended 31 March 2026 [Q1/2026], up 6.1% from the previous quarter [Q4/2025] and up 27% from the year-earlier period [Q1/2025].

Major changes compared with previous corresponding period [Mar 31, 2026 vs Mar 31, 2025]:

Favourable Changes:

- Net profit was up 27.2% from $2.0b to $2.5b

- Total revenue was up 22.2% from $14.2b to $17.4b

- EPS growth 30.3% from $4.22 to $5.5

- EBIT to Sales was up from 18% to 18.4%

- Profit before tax to Sales was up from 18% to 18.4%

- Profit after tax to Sales was up from 14.1% to 14.6%

Note:

- Tax expenses to Sales was down from 4% to 3.8%

urn:contentItem:6JGG-8H13-RRXY-F1KX-00000-00CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - RECENT PEER AND INDUSTRY TRENDS2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:10ZLexisNexisSTOCKCLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY, INC. - RECENT PEER AND INDUSTRY TRENDS

DASHBOARD: CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY

PermID4296495921Websitehttp://www.claycorp.comIndustryConstructionAddress2199 Innerbelt Business Center Drive Overland, Missouri 63114

ACTIVITIES:

Clayco Construction Company, Inc., founded in 1984 by Bob Clark, is a design-build firm headquartered in St. Louis, Missouri. With over 3,800 employees and $7.6 billion in 2024 revenue, it serves commercial, industrial, and life sciences sectors. Clayco offers architecture, engineering, and construction services through subsidiaries like Lamar Johnson Collaborative and Concrete Strategies. Known for projects like the Novus Headquarters, it emphasizes sustainable, LEED-certified buildings. In 2024, Clayco expanded into renewable energy and data center construction. Its culture fosters innovation, safety, and diversity, with robust training programs. Competing with DPR Construction, Clayco stands out for its integrated approach and fast-track delivery. Recent initiatives include advancing technology-driven construction and eco-friendly practices.

+ CLAYCO CONSTRUCTION COMPANY [3 News That Matters]

+ CHICAGO BUILDER CLAYCO BETS ON SOLAR AND STORAGE WITH NEW NATIONAL ARM

Clayco, a Chicago-based contractor, is expanding into solar and storage with a new national division, announced on 4th March 2026. The venture, known as Clayco Power and Energy, aims to create nearly 1,000 jobs nationwide by 2027 and generate about $300 million in annual revenue.The unit will oversee design, engineering, construction, and procurement for solar and storage projects and is already advancing a 350-acre Manteno, Illinois site, with construction slated to begin this June.The expansion comes as U.S. electricity demand is forecast to rise 15%–20% by 2030, with solar and storage contributing a growing share of new capacity after accounting for roughly 85% of last year’s additions.

+ IDAHO NATIONAL LAB TO HOST NUCLEAR-POWERED AI DATA CENTER: CLAYCO TO LEAD DOE PROPOSAL DESIGN-BUILD EFFORT

Clayco, a leading design-build firm specializing in complex mission-critical facilities, has announced its collaboration with Swiss-American energy company Deep Atomic to support submissions to the U.S. Department of Energy for a nuclear-powered AI data center campus planned at Idaho National Laboratory, a project that could usher in a new era of energy-efficient computing.On 12th February 2026, Clayco said it would provide early-stage delivery planning, constructability analysis, and integrated design-build expertise as part of the consortium. The firm’s track record in delivering large-scale data centers positions it to help ensure the campus can be designed and built safely, efficiently, and at scale. Bob Clark, Executive Chairman and Founder of Clayco, stressed that confidence in project delivery is essential for a successful DOE submission.In its advisory role, Clayco will align design and construction approaches with the operational requirements of high-density AI workloads, leveraging its integrated delivery model to meet the fast, reliable, and precise needs of modern data centers. The consortium brings together organizations with expertise in advanced nuclear technology, energy systems, and risk modeling, complementing Clayco's practical delivery capabilities. Deep Atomic's approach combines advanced nuclear energy with AI data center infrastructure to ensure reliable, low-carbon power for next-generation computing. The collaboration aims to establish a scalable platform that could position the U.S. as a leader in clean-energy AI infrastructure; Deep Atomic founder and CEO William G. J. Theron said the mission is to accelerate deployment of advanced nuclear power to meet performance and sustainability needs, with Clayco helping integrate innovative energy solutions and execution planning for the DOE submission.

+ FOUNTAIN PARK AS BEACON OF RENEWAL: CLAYCO'S CLARK CALLS FOR COLLABORATIVE RECOVERY

Nearly a year after the May 16 tornado devastated North St. Louis, Clayco CEO Bob Clark says the business community must join with city leaders to forge a unified rebuilding strategy for areas that faced long-standing struggles. In Fountain Park, he voiced cautious optimism about renewal and noted the visible damage from the disaster.Clark said Clayco acted promptly after the storm, coordinating with Mayor Spencer, city services, and local partners. The company’s commitment to long-term rebuilding includes funding and staffing programs with Albion and Washington University to improve quality of life and keep the city moving forward, complemented by ongoing community support.Describing Fountain Park as a potential beacon of hope, Clark highlighted preexisting programs that promote new housing and help families find safe, affordable homes, such as the St. Louis Development Corporation and the Land Clearance for Redevelopment Authority. He emphasized that real recovery starts with the civic community and requires a shared will to move forward, adding that progress happens incrementally—one house at a time, one block at a time.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

+ RYAN COMPANIES [2 News That Matters]

+ MERITAX ADVISORS JOINS RYAN COMPANIES TO ACCELERATE PROPERTY TAX SOLUTIONS

Ryan Companies expanded its property tax services with the acquisition of Meritax Advisors, announced official on the 10th of February 2026. Meritax, a Texas-based commercial property tax consultant, has supported clients across various real estate sectors and manages tens of billions of dollars in assessed value in Texas and beyond.The deal is designed to boost Ryan's capabilities in valuation strategy, litigation management and large-portfolio advisory services, while incorporating Meritax's analytics-driven approach and technology-enabled advocacy to keep pace with evolving property tax systems. Damon Chronis, President of U.S. Operations at Ryan, said the addition brings a disciplined, client-focused team with deep Texas knowledge to the firm.As part of the integration, Meritax partners including Ryan Chismark, Charlie Honea and Mark Kline, along with about 20 other team members, will join Ryan. The move adds to Ryan’s global footprint, which already serves more than 74,000 clients with a workforce of over 7,100 professionals, reinforcing the firm’s leading position in business taxes.

+ VETERANS DAY FEATURE: ANNA GRIFFIN CONNECTS MILITARY SERVICE TO CONSTRUCTION EXCELLENCE AT RYAN

Anna Griffin, director of construction for Ryan Companies US, sat down on the AZ Big Podcast to discuss how her military background informs leadership and resilience in the construction industry. The interview, recorded 5 days ago, comes as Veterans Day is observed in Arizona and highlights Griffin's trajectory from Air Force service to overseeing Southwest construction projects. Griffin recently returned from a seven-month deployment to Jordan as aircraft maintenance squadron commander for the 165th Airlift Wing, and she notes that the two fields share common ground: "Construction has so many parallels to my military service," she noted. "You’re still serving — just in a different way. You’re building something lasting, something that supports people and communities." At Ryan Companies, Griffin guides project management teams across healthcare, multifamily housing, and education. After nearly four years at the firm, her leadership helps navigate the complexity of construction environments, with a focus on integrity, accountability, and mission readiness, qualities that served her in the military and now guide civilian projects. The AZ Big Podcast, hosted by Michael Gossie and Amy Lindsey, is sponsored by Burch & Cracchiolo and aims to spotlight how strong leadership and collaboration drive success across industries in Arizona's business landscape. Griffin's episode is timely as the state honors its veterans and recognizes the valuable skills they contribute to the workforce. Listeners are encouraged to tune in to hear her compelling story and understand how veterans like her are making meaningful contributions to sectors such as construction. As she continues her work with Ryan Companies, her journey demonstrates the strength and adaptability of veterans transitioning into fresh roles, underscoring that the capabilities developed in military service are transferable across fields.

++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

urn:contentItem:6JGJ-DM33-SJPN-H15K-00000-00Solar booms in industrial US midwest as energy crisis persists2026-05-02T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisUS NEWSVersion:1Solar booms in industrial US midwest as energy crisis persistsStephen Starr in Lima, Ohio

Electricity has become one of the most important commodities in the region thanks to demand from datacenters, Iran war and rising utility charges

For decades, the only regular visitors to the Twin Lake Reservoir in Lima, Ohio, were fishers passing hot summer evenings trying to snag a largemouth bass.

But today, it’s a hive of activity.

A team of 12 engineers and construction workers are busily connecting more than 3,400 solar arrays to small, floating docks and distributing them across four acres of the reservoir’s surface water.

The electricity generated by the floating photovoltaics will be used to power a nearby water treatment plant, where electricity-powered pumps run 24 hours a day, year-round.

“The water treatment plant is one of the city’s biggest energy costs; it only made sense to put the floating solar site here,” says Sara Weekley, deputy director of Lima’s utilities department.

“It also helps keep water rates stable by lowering energy costs.”

The project is part of an emerging evolution in the industrial midwest from heavy manufacturing to clean energy.

Electricity has turned into one of the most important commodities in the region, with utility rates increasing in recent years due to demand from datacenters, rising utility charges and the war on Iran, which has driven gas pump prices to $5 a gallon locally.

Consumers have been crying out for alternatives. And while floating solar projects are available only to those with access to waterways, states such as Michigan and Minnesota have some of the highest numbers of lakes in the country, not counting the massive Great Lakes system that borders Ohio and seven other US states.

The Lima floating solar project is the work of Florida-headquartered D3Energy , which, with more than 25 projects underway, has built more floating solar arrays in the US than any other company. Ninety miles northwest of Lima, the company has just finished building a floating solar project three times larger than this.

“Across most of the midwest, and in Ohio in particular, agricultural land is a critical piece of the economy – you don’t want renewable energy and food production fighting each other for the same acres,” says Stetson Tchividjian, D3Energy’s managing director.

“Floating solar resolves that equation.”

In winter, when temperatures fall below freezing, water from neighboring reservoirs is pumped in to prevent ice forming, which allows the solar arrays to continue providing electricity uninterrupted.

“Floating solar has a significantly smaller footprint than ground-mounted solar. A typical one megawatt floating solar system can fit on roughly two acres of water, compared to approximately five acres of land for a comparable ground-mounted system,” says Tchividjian.

Despite a reputation for being cloudy and grey, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, Ohio gets more sun than Oregon and almost as much as Alabama, a state hundreds of miles further south.

According to the Solar Energy Industries Association, midwestern states Illinois, Indiana and Ohio respectivelyrank 10th, 11th and 12th nationally for solar capacity.

An hour’s drive north in Perrysburg, Ohio, First Solar has become one of the largest solar module manufacturers in the western hemisphere. Two years ago, it opened the largest solar R&D facility in the country, where it has spent $2.4bn manufacturingperovskite semiconductor-built solar technology that’s expected to usher in a new world of higher-efficiency solar energy applications.

“What it’s done is remind people why energy independence matters,” Tchividjian says of how the rise of solar applications in the midwest is simultaneously happening when 20% of the world’s oil consumption is blocked in the strait of Hormuz.

“Domestic generation that isn’t exposed to a single geopolitical event is more valuable, not less, in this kind of environment. Solar is a key cog in that wheel.”

All this is happening in the heart of the eastern corn belt where farmers, like elsewhere, are struggling to make a profit from growing crops.

For landowners and farmers, solar leases have become a route to guaranteed income at a time when the Trump administration’s tariffs regime is destroying demand for US agricultural products such as soybeans in China and beyond.

Still, solar faces reputational challenges in rural America.

The idea of huge sheets of glass and metal covering land that for centuries has provided food for millions of Americans and people around the world doesn’t sit easily with everyone.

In March, a tornado 180 miles west in neighboring Indianadestroyed large parts of one of the largest solar arrays east of the Mississippi River, and which could cost hundreds of millions of dollars to repair, illustrating for some the inherent vulnerability of the technology.

For Doug Goyings and his family, who farm about 5,000 acres of barley, soybeans and corn in Paulding county, an hour’s drive north-west of the floating solar project in Lima, the solar experience – two acres of arrays that put out 130 kilowatts of power – has not been a good one.

“I don’t have to pay an electric bill at all, on the generation side. But the transmission and distribution [charges], it’s outrageous. They’re saving us all this money, but the electric company is not going to take a loss. They got these fees that they put on there,” he says.

Goyings recalls that in March, he produced 2,160 kilowatts extra that went back to the utility’s – American Electric Power (AEP) – grid. But doing so saw him charged $918 in transmission and distribution fees.

“I used no electricity from them,” he says.

Fossil fuel companies have spent millions of dollars onscare and disinformation campaigns that have resulted in city and county authorities banning large-scale solar farms from being constructed on agricultural land across the region. In 2024, AEPgenerated around 3.5 times as much electricity from coal and natural gas as from renewables.

Some rural residents oppose solar farms due to their alleged role in inflating the price of agricultural land for crop growers and farmers.

But others disagree.

“The tension around solar is often less about the technology and more about change to communities,” says Jeff Risley, executive director of Renewable Energy Farmers of America, a nonprofit trade association.

“Many landowners feel caught in the middle: developers want their signature; neighbors may be hostile and there is limited independent guidance to help them evaluate whether a project makes sense for their situation.”

And of the Indiana tornado incident, Risley believes that localized weather events don’t amount to systems fragility.

“The industry has developedtechnology specifically to protect against severe weather,” he says.

“They now have tracking systems with ‘stow’ modes that position panels at steep angles to reduce hail and wind damage.”

In Lima, a city better known for refining oil and building military tanks, the floating solar project on a reservoir that supplies thousands of residents with drinking water, marks a major shift.

When the project is finished this summer, it’s estimated to save the city and taxpayers around $10m over the course of its lifetime. On top of that, the solar arrays will help lower evaporation rates and algae growth in the water by providing a barrier to sunlight.

“It keeps the water cooler; we’re not using any additional land,” says Weekley.

“People like the idea that it’s not taking up any land and of us trying to save money.”

urn:contentItem:6JK9-6YK3-RSX4-R561-00000-00Negating Neighborhood Noise Act Legislation by Rep. Williams Analyzed2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisNegating Neighborhood Noise Act Legislation by Rep. Williams AnalyzedBailey Malota, Targeted News Service

WASHINGTON, May 14 -- The Negating Neighborhood Noise Act, originally introduced by Rep. Nikema Williams, D-GA, on May 7, 2026, has been analyzed by the Congressional Research Service. The bill seeks to amend the National Highway System Designation Act of 1995 to allow for the construction of specific noise barriers using funds from the Highway Trust Fund, thereby addressing the community's need for noise mitigation along major highways.

This legislation arises from growing concerns over rising noise pollution in residential areas adjacent to highways. As urban populations continue to expand, many communities experience significant disturbances from traffic noise, which can adversely affect residents' quality of life, health, and property values. By enabling the construction of Type II noise barriers-designed to mitigate highway noise-the bill aims to improve living conditions for affected populations.

One noteworthy feature of the bill is its provision for multipurpose noise barriers. The Secretary of Transportation will oversee that these barriers not only reduce noise but also have secondary beneficial uses, such as hosting renewable energy generation systems or broadband infrastructure. This aspect reflects a creative approach to maximize the utility of infrastructure investments, potentially leading to enhanced economic and environmental outcomes.

Furthermore, the bill emphasizes aesthetics in the design of new noise barriers, requiring compliance with guidelines from the Federal Highway Administration's Noise Barrier Design Handbook. This focus on visual impact ensures that noise mitigation solutions are not only functional but also considerate of community aesthetics.

As discussions continue around urban planning and environmental standards, the Negating Neighborhood Noise Act represents a significant step toward addressing the challenges posed by noise pollution while fostering innovation in infrastructure development.

The bill (H.R. 8717) has 3 co-sponsors: Reps. James A. Himes, D-CT; Eleanor Holmes Norton, D-DC; Sean Casten, D-IL.

value.Overview: Daily Ledger (Illinois), NEWS; Pg. E3, 507words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-F203-S4JX-P4VW-00000-00Illinois boosts film industry growth with new green tax incentive2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. E3Illinois boosts film industry growth with new green tax incentiveByJanis ReeserSpringfield State Journal-Register

Illinois film and television productions that meet green certification standards can now earn an extra 5% tax credit, making the state the first in the nation to offer such an incentive, according to a community announcement.

The initiative, launched in celebration of Earth Day, is expected to boost both the entertainment industry and the state's green economy, according to the announcement.

Productions recognized as a "certified green production" through the Illinois Film Office must meet environmental benchmarks such as reducing waste, lowering energy use and emissions, utilizing sustainable materials and implementing responsible food practices.

Illinois film industry sees record growth

The announcement comes as Illinois' film industry continues to grow, with production expenditures reaching a record-breaking $703 million in 2025 and supporting an estimated 18,000 industry hires. The expansion of the tax credit is expected to drive demand for clean energy infrastructure, such as battery systems, solar-powered trailers and renewable energy solutions, creating new jobs across the state.

Chicago now ranks as the third-best big city for those who work in the film industry, according to the announcement.

Officials applaud Illinois film industry expansion

"This expansion reflects our commitment to growing Illinois' economy in a way that is both competitive and sustainable," Kristin Richards, director of the Illinois Department of Commerce and Economic Opportunity, said in the announcement. "We are attracting world-class productions while building the infrastructure and workforce needed for a cleaner future."

The new incentive is also expected to drive growth in Illinois' clean energy and green vendor sectors. As productions adapt to meet certification requirements, demand will increase for infrastructure such as battery systems, solar-powered trailers and renewable energy solutions - in turn creating new, good-paying jobs across the state.

"As someone who has worked for years to help make productions in Illinois more sustainable, I am absolutely thrilled that Governor Pritzker and the Illinois Film Office have made Illinois the first state in the country to offer productions a meaningful incentive to go green," Ellen White, CEO and founder of Ecofixr, an Illinois-based full-service sustainability services company, said in the announcement. "This is going to be a gamechanger for Illinois and will undoubtedly help Illinois attract more productions and the green jobs that go with them."

In addition to long-term infrastructure growth, more immediate changes on set are expected, such as transitioning from paper scripts to digital tablets, replacing single-use plastic water bottles with refill stations, and reducing reliance on diesel in favor of cleaner energy alternatives.

urn:contentItem:6JJW-YGM3-RYR5-84D2-00000-00Landmark Michigan Appellate Decision Upholds Limits on Local Regulation of Renewable Energy Projects2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisLandmark Michigan Appellate Decision Upholds Limits on Local Regulation of Renewable Energy Projects

The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

ArentFox Schiff Amy Antoniolli Meera Gorjala Robert Middleton Duncan Weinstein

May 13th, 2026 ( JD Supra - Delivered by Newstex )

In a recent win for renewable energy developers, the Michigan Court of Appeals confirmed that Michigan law prohibits local governments from imposing requirements on wind, solar, and energy storage projects more restrictive than state law.

The decision comes on the heels of a similar appellate decision in Illinois upholding another 'no more restrictive than' state-level zoning structure. The Michigan decision largely affirms the Michigan Public Service Commission's (PSC) interpretation of a 2023 state law, effective November 29, 2024, that restructured the local approval process for renewable energy developments. The ruling provides important clarity for local governments and renewable energy developers planning projects.

Background

Enacted in 2023, Michigan Public Act 233 (PA 233) transformed siting for solar energy, wind energy, and energy storage systems. The law does not completely eliminate local zoning but shifts authority from local governments to the state by allowing developers to obtain a siting certificate from the PSC if projects meet certain capacity thresholds.

The law authorizes local governments to continue to regulate siting for qualifying projects only if the local government adopts an ordinance compatible with the new law. In practice, this means that local governments cannot adopt requirements more restrictive than state law standards. If an applicant seeks a certificate from the PSC and meets the statutory criteria, the PSC 'shall grant' approval. A certificate from the PSC preempts local siting law, though projects must still comply with local building or operating permit requirements.

The Lawsuit

A coalition of 79 townships and counties filed suit, arguing that the PSC's regulations implementing the new law exceeded its authority by redefining statutory terms, creating a new category of facilities, modifying statutory timelines, and implementing a rule without proper administrative rulemaking procedures. The local governments argued that their own siting ordinances could still include requirements that did not overlap with state law requirements.

The Court's Decision

The court upheld the PSC's interpretation that local siting ordinances 'may only contain the setback, fencing, height, sound, and other applicable requirements expressly outlined in [Michigan Code of Laws 460.1226(8)] and may not contain additional requirements more restrictive than those specifically identified in that section.' The court reasoned that allowing local governments to adopt requirements in addition to those imposed under state law 'would inherently be more restrictive,' which would conflict with the legislature's intent.

Key Takeaways

Local ordinances in Michigan cannot exceed state standards. The court affirmed that local governments cannot impose requirements more restrictive than those under state law. If a local government attempts to do so, developers may seek a PSC certificate as an alternative.

This is a growing trend. Like Michigan, an Illinois appellate court recently affirmed that a 2023 Illinois law requires counties to approve siting requests for solar projects that meet state law requirements, precluding counties from imposing more restrictive requirements.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-J7R3-RS50-S405-00000-00Rexel USA (OTC: RXEEY (ADR)) Acquires Revere Electric Supply to Expand Midwest Presence2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisRexel USA (OTC: RXEEY (ADR)) Acquires Revere Electric Supply to Expand Midwest Presence

DAILY ADR AND DEPOSITORY RECEIPT REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

13 May 2026 15:01 EDT

Rexel USA (OTC: RXEEY (ADR)) completes the acquisition of Revere Electric Supply, a prominent electrical distributor based in Mokena, Illinois. The deal closed on May 13, 2026, and expands Rexel USA's regional footprint by aligning with a service-oriented supplier known for strong customer relationships and technical expertise.

Founded in 1919, Revere Electric Supply operates 10 locations across NorthernIllinois, Central Illinois, Southeast Wisconsin, and Northwest Indiana, and employs 375 people. The company specializes in industrial automation and maintains long-standing relationships with electrical contractors, underpinning its standing in the Midwest industrial market.

Under the arrangement, Revere will retain its name and local identity, ensuring continuity for customers and partners. Rexel USA will preserve Revere's culture and leadership team, with John Cady continuing as President and taking on expanded responsibilities for Rexel's broader Midwest operations, supported by Rexel Midwest Region President Hani Masoudi. Rexel USA Chief Executive Roger Little framed the acquisition as a strategic move to strengthen the company's position in a critical geographic area, while acknowledging Revere's capabilities and customer focus.

IN TODAY'S REPORT:

SECTION 1 REXEL ACTIVITIES

SECTION 1 Rexel Activities

Activities

Rexel is a leading global distributor of electrical, automation, and digital solutions, headquartered in France, with operations spanning over 30 countries. The company's core business activities center on supplying a wide range of products, including electrical wires, cables, lighting, HVAC systems, and automation technologies, primarily to professionals in the construction, industrial, and services sectors. It also provides value-added services such as technical consulting, project management, and digital tools to enhance efficiency for its customers. Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy solutions and digital platforms, fueled by rising demand for sustainable technologies and smart building systems. This growth is supported by Rexel's strategic investments in e-commerce and energy management, positioning the company to capitalize on global trends toward electrification and decarbonization. It is the International Depository Receipt (IDR) market's 10th largest Electronics company by market capitalisation.

value.Title: Illinois boosts film industry growth with new green tax incentive
value.Overview: Daily Ledger (Illinois), NEWS; Pg. A1, 507words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJT-FTG3-SBHV-G3FD-00000-00Illinois boosts film industry growth with new green tax incentive2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. A1Illinois boosts film industry growth with new green tax incentiveByJanis ReeserSpringfield State Journal-Register

Illinois film and television productions that meet green certification standards can now earn an extra 5% tax credit, making the state the first in the nation to offer such an incentive, according to a community announcement.

The initiative, launched in celebration of Earth Day, is expected to boost both the entertainment industry and the state's green economy, according to the announcement.

Productions recognized as a "certified green production" through the Illinois Film Office must meet environmental benchmarks such as reducing waste, lowering energy use and emissions, utilizing sustainable materials and implementing responsible food practices.

Illinois film industry

sees record growth

The announcement comes as Illinois' film industry continues to grow, with production expenditures reaching a record-breaking $703 million in 2025 and supporting an estimated 18,000 industry hires. The expansion of the tax credit is expected to drive demand for clean energy infrastructure, such as battery systems, solar-powered trailers and renewable energy solutions, creating new jobs across the state.

Chicago now ranks as the third-best big city for those who work in the film industry, according to the announcement.

Officials applaud Illinois

film industry expansion

"This expansion reflects our commitment to growing Illinois' economy in a way that is both competitive and sustainable," Kristin Richards, director of the Illinois Department of Commerce and Economic Opportunity, said in the announcement. "We are attracting world-class productions while building the infrastructure and workforce needed for a cleaner future."

The new incentive is also expected to drive growth in Illinois' clean energy and green vendor sectors. As productions adapt to meet certification requirements, demand will increase for infrastructure such as battery systems, solar-powered trailers and renewable energy solutions - in turn creating new, good-paying jobs across the state.

"As someone who has worked for years to help make productions in Illinois more sustainable, I am absolutely thrilled that Governor Pritzker and the Illinois Film Office have made Illinois the first state in the country to offer productions a meaningful incentive to go green," Ellen White, CEO and founder of Ecofixr, an Illinois-based full-service sustainability services company, said in the announcement. "This is going to be a gamechanger for Illinois and will undoubtedly help Illinois attract more productions and the green jobs that go with them."

In addition to long-term infrastructure growth, more immediate changes on set are expected, such as transitioning from paper scripts to digital tablets, replacing single-use plastic water bottles with refill stations, and reducing reliance on diesel in favor of cleaner energy alternatives.

This story was created with the assistance of Artificial Intelligence (AI). Journalists were involved in every step of the information gathering, review, editing and publishing process. Learn more at https://cm.usatoday.com/ethical-conduct/.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHM-8873-SHNH-22JJ-00000-00DTE Energy Company's 5-Year Earnings Growth Lags Exelon Corporation2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company's 5-Year Earnings Growth Lags Exelon Corporation

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been -0.1% annualized over 5 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 36.7% or 9.5% annualized.

In comparison, Exelon Corporation's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 13.8% annualized over 5 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 72.4% or 15.2% annualized.

DTE Energy Company's last trading price was $143.50 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 22.2. With an EPS growth rate of 8.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 2.66 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Exelon Corporation's last trading price was $46.18 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 16.9. With an EPS growth rate of 9.6 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 1.75 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

Company Profile

Exelon Corporation operates as a holding company for regulated electric and gas utilities, primarily generating revenue through electricity transmission and distribution, which accounts for approximately 85% of total revenue. The largest revenue contributors are its PHI subsidiary, focused on Mid-Atlantic operations, and ComEd, serving northern Illinois, which together represent over 58% of the company's $24.3 billion full-year revenue. The fastest-growing segments include BGE, driven by Maryland distribution rates, and PECO, supported by Pennsylvania electric and gas rates and favorable weather conditions. These segments have shown significant year-over-year growth, reflecting the company's investments in infrastructure and regulatory recoveries.

value.Title: IL boosts film industry growth with green tax incentive
value.Overview: Daily Leader (Illinois), NEWS; Pg. A1, 507words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-KVP3-S221-V30K-00000-00IL boosts film industry growth with green tax incentive2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. A1IL boosts film industry growth with green tax incentiveByJanis ReeserSpringfield State Journal-Register

Illinois film and television productions that meet green certification standards can now earn an extra 5% tax credit, making the state the first in the nation to offer such an incentive, according to a community announcement.

The initiative, launched in celebration of Earth Day, is expected to boost both the entertainment industry and the state's green economy, according to the announcement.

Productions recognized as a "certified green production" through the Illinois Film Office must meet environmental benchmarks such as reducing waste, lowering energy use and emissions, utilizing sustainable materials and implementing responsible food practices.

Illinois film industry

sees record growth

The announcement comes as Illinois' film industry continues to grow, with production expenditures reaching a record-breaking $703 million in 2025 and supporting an estimated 18,000 industry hires. The expansion of the tax credit is expected to drive demand for clean energy infrastructure, such as battery systems, solar-powered trailers and renewable energy solutions, creating new jobs across the state.

Chicago now ranks as the third-best big city for those who work in the film industry, according to the announcement.

Officials applaud Illinois

film industry expansion

"This expansion reflects our commitment to growing Illinois' economy in a way that is both competitive and sustainable," Kristin Richards, director of the Illinois Department of Commerce and Economic Opportunity, said in the announcement. "We are attracting world-class productions while building the infrastructure and workforce needed for a cleaner future."

The new incentive is also expected to drive growth in Illinois' clean energy and green vendor sectors. As productions adapt to meet certification requirements, demand will increase for infrastructure such as battery systems, solar-powered trailers and renewable energy solutions - in turn creating new, good-paying jobs across the state.

"As someone who has worked for years to help make productions in Illinois more sustainable, I am absolutely thrilled that Governor Pritzker and the Illinois Film Office have made Illinois the first state in the country to offer productions a meaningful incentive to go green," Ellen White, CEO and founder of Ecofixr, an Illinois-based full-service sustainability services company, said in the announcement. "This is going to be a gamechanger for Illinois and will undoubtedly help Illinois attract more productions and the green jobs that go with them."

In addition to long-term infrastructure growth, more immediate changes on set are expected, such as transitioning from paper scripts to digital tablets, replacing single-use plastic water bottles with refill stations, and reducing reliance on diesel in favor of cleaner energy alternatives.

This story was created with the assistance of Artificial Intelligence (AI). Journalists were involved in every step of the information gathering, review, editing and publishing process. Learn more at https://cm.usatoday.com/ethical-conduct/.

value.Title: DTE Energy Company 's 3-Year Earnings Growth Lags Exelon Corporation
value.Overview: Global Round Up - Sectors, 872words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHC-YV13-SHN4-4267-00000-00DTE Energy Company's 3-Year Earnings Growth Lags Exelon Corporation2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company's 3-Year Earnings Growth Lags Exelon Corporation

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 8.4% annualized over 3 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 41.5% or 14.9% annualized.

In comparison, Exelon Corporation's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 9.6% annualized over 3 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 20.7% or 9.7% annualized.

DTE Energy Company's last trading price was $146.73 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 20.8. With an EPS growth rate of 8.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 2.49 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Exelon Corporation's last trading price was $46.61 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 17. With an EPS growth rate of 9.6 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 1.77 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

Company Profile

Exelon Corporation operates as a holding company for regulated electric and gas utilities, primarily generating revenue through electricity transmission and distribution, which accounts for approximately 85% of total revenue. The largest revenue contributors are its PHI subsidiary, focused on Mid-Atlantic operations, and ComEd, serving northern Illinois, which together represent over 58% of the company's $24.3 billion full-year revenue. The fastest-growing segments include BGE, driven by Maryland distribution rates, and PECO, supported by Pennsylvania electric and gas rates and favorable weather conditions. These segments have shown significant year-over-year growth, reflecting the company's investments in infrastructure and regulatory recoveries.

value.Title: IL boosts film industry growth with green tax incentive
value.Overview: The Journal Standard (Illinois), NEWS; Pg. A2, 507words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-NGW3-S8R9-X18K-00000-00IL boosts film industry growth with green tax incentive2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. A2IL boosts film industry growth with green tax incentiveByJanis ReeserRockford Register Star

Illinois film and television productions that meet green certification standards can now earn an extra 5% tax credit, making the state the first in the nation to offer such an incentive, according to a community announcement.

The initiative, launched in celebration of Earth Day, is expected to boost both the entertainment industry and the state's green economy, according to the announcement.

Productions recognized as a "certified green production" through the Illinois Film Office must meet environmental benchmarks such as reducing waste, lowering energy use and emissions, utilizing sustainable materials and implementing responsible food practices.

Illinois film industry

sees record growth

The announcement comes as Illinois' film industry continues to grow, with production expenditures reaching a record-breaking $703 million in 2025 and supporting an estimated 18,000 industry hires. The expansion of the tax credit is expected to drive demand for clean energy infrastructure, such as battery systems, solar-powered trailers and renewable energy solutions, creating new jobs across the state.

Chicago now ranks as the third-best big city for those who work in the film industry, according to the announcement.

Officials applaud Illinois

film industry expansion

"This expansion reflects our commitment to growing Illinois' economy in a way that is both competitive and sustainable," Kristin Richards, director of the Illinois Department of Commerce and Economic Opportunity, said in the announcement. "We are attracting world-class productions while building the infrastructure and workforce needed for a cleaner future."

The new incentive is also expected to drive growth in Illinois' clean energy and green vendor sectors. As productions adapt to meet certification requirements, demand will increase for infrastructure such as battery systems, solar-powered trailers and renewable energy solutions, in turn creating new, good-paying jobs across the state.

"As someone who has worked for years to help make productions in Illinois more sustainable, I am absolutely thrilled that Governor Pritzker and the Illinois Film Office have made Illinois the first state in the country to offer productions a meaningful incentive to go green," Ellen White, CEO and founder of Ecofixr, an Illinois-based full-service sustainability services company, said in the announcement. "This is going to be a gamechanger for Illinois and will undoubtedly help Illinois attract more productions and the green jobs that go with them."

In addition to long-term infrastructure growth, more immediate changes on set are expected, such as transitioning from paper scripts to digital tablets, replacing single-use plastic water bottles with refill stations, and reducing reliance on diesel in favor of cleaner energy alternatives.

This story was created with the assistance of artificial intelligence (AI). Journalists were involved in every step of the information gathering, review, editing and publishing process. Learn more at https://cm.usatoday.com/ethical-conduct/.

value.Title: The Illinois Energy Agency is returning $580 million to ComEd customers: everything you need to know
value.Overview: CE Noticias Financieras English, 497words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGR-KHJ3-S68D-8035-00000-00The Illinois Energy Agency is returning $580 million to ComEd customers: everything you need to know2026-05-02T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisThe Illinois Energy Agency is returning $580 million to ComEd customers: everything you need to know


In the first three months of 2026, the Illinois Power Authority (IPA) returned $580 million to ComEd (Commonwealth Edison) customersas a result of the Carbon Mitigation Credits (CMC) initiative. This refund is crucial at a time when consumers need relief from rising electricity costs.


CMCs represent the environmental benefits of certain nuclear power generation facilities. The CMC pricing structure is such that their sale finances the operation of nuclear power plants through payments when wholesale energy and capacity prices are low.
In these cases, the law requires operators to return a credit to the purchasing utility (and, ultimately, to customers) when energy prices are high.
This is not the first time the IPA (Illinois Power Authority) has made such a refund. Since 2022, ComEd customers have collectively received more than $1.868 billion in electricity bill credits under the CMC initiative. This figure is expected to continue rising, according to the state agency's projections.
What are the actual savings on bills?
The agency reports that "in January 2026 alone, $404 million was distributed directly to residential, commercial, and industrial customers as a separate line item on their electricity bills. Over the life of the program, some 3.8 million ComEd residential customers have saved an average of $177 each."
Carbon credits are linked to revenue from nuclear power plants./ Photo: IPA.
"The CMC initiative continues to deliver significant benefits to ComEd customers by reducing costs," said Brian Granahan, director of the IPA. He added that "this program has significantly supported clean energy in Illinois, while protecting customers from sudden rate hikes."
It all began in 2021, when the Climate and Equitable Jobs Act (CEJA) allowed for the creation of a program to support nuclear power plants by facilitating the purchase of CMCs at a price that guarantees their revenue security. During periods of low energy prices, this purchase can function as a much-needed subsidy to maintain the plants.
During periods of high prices, plants participating in the program are required to return surplus revenue to ComEd. The credits on the bill are also, in part, the result of federal tax credits received by the nuclear plants.
The Illinois Power Authority (IPA) was established under Illinois state law in 2007. Its primary function is the development and implementation of energy procurement plans for residential customers and small businesses.
It is also responsible for developing and implementing other procurement plans, including those aimed at supporting the development of renewable energy and the continued operation of nuclear power plants.
Both Illinois Governor J.B. Pritzker (right) and Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson are fighting for clean energy in Illinois./ Photo: X.com/ @GovPritzker.
The IPA administers incentive programs and conducts procurements to promote renewable and carbon-free energy generation for the benefit of all Illinois residents.

value.Title: Dire need for renewables
value.Overview: Chicago Tribune, OPINION; Pg. B3, 1498words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ7-CGY3-S31K-41TW-00000-00Dire need for renewables2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:49:29ZLexisNexisOPINIONPg. B3Dire need for renewables

Photo Caption: Wind turbines generating renewable energy rise above farmland Aug. 20, 2025, in Gibson City. John J. Kim/Tribune

Dire need for renewables

Regarding the editorial "Owner of massive Elwood Energy plant isn't waiting for Illinois closure deadlines. They're literally moving the plant to Texas" (April 17): Amid news that Hull Street Energy planned to load six gas turbines onto flatbeds and drive them to Texas, clean energy detractors were quick to point to the Climate and Equitable Jobs Act (CEJA) as responsible. That's false. In fact, the Elwood gas plant is a perfect case study in the dire need to replace aging, expensive fossil fuel plants with reliable and cost-effective energy resources like solar, wind and battery storage.

Over the past seven years, Elwood has hardly operated. It runs less than 5% of the time and, when running, costs $75 per megawatt-hour, or five times the price of energy from new solar generation. Elwood is also unreliable. During Winter Storm Elliott, Elwood was one of eight gas plants in Illinois that failed to perform when called upon by PJM and was subject to "nonperformance" financial penalties. With high operating costs and poor performance, it's not surprising that J-Power announced that it would close the plant in 2025, well before the 2030 CEJA deadline.

Then, Hull Street Energy found a loophole and made a short-sighted investment deal. It is now scrapping the plant for parts. Texas is willing to take the nearly 30-year-old turbines because there's currently a five- to seven-year wait to purchase new gas turbines. Texas (and its deregulated energy market) is effectively ending up with our polluting rubble because of supply chain issues.

Meanwhile, the real culprit of our skyrocketing energy bills is moving in next door to the Elwood gas plant. Two out-of-state corporations, Hillwood Investment Properties and PowerHouse Data Centers, have proposed building the "Joliet Technology Center" - a 1.8-gigawatt data center that will sit on 795 acres next to Elwood. Data centers like these are creating a new energy era, driving unprecedented electricity demand. Instead of throwing in the towel on our CEJA goals, we have more reason than ever to double down on producing cheaper clean energy and ensure data centers don't drive up energy costs while polluting our communities.

Until data center developers are required to bring their own clean energy and capacity resources and are held accountable for their outsized impact on the grid, the strain on our power grid will be untenable. It's time to pass the POWER Act (SB4016/HB5513) and ensure the new energy era builds on CEJA, delivering a clean energy future for Illinois communities, consumers, businesses and even data centers.

- Jen Walling, CEO, Illinois Environmental Council

Coverage of gambling ring

Recently, the Tribune's Post-Tribune newspaper characterized a gambling ring in Indiana as "Greek-led" - a phrase that fills me with profound disappointment. Such language is not only superfluous but also perpetuates toxic stereotypes, especially when news outlets regularly omit similar ethnic identifiers for criminal groups led by individuals who belong to other ethnic backgrounds.

Coverage of high-profile cases involving the mafia, for example, rarely foregrounds ethnicity; instead, journalists focus on actions and facts. Crimes involving individuals of color are similarly reported without highlighting their backgrounds unless it holds genuine relevance to the story.

By specifically spotlighting Greek heritage in this instance, the Tribune draws unwarranted attention to ethnicity, as if it were a defining element of the crime rather than incidental. This selective reporting fortifies the misguided notion that certain groups are inherently predisposed to criminal behavior - a notion rooted in antiquated prejudice and patently untrue. Moreover, it undermines the hard-won progress toward recognizing crime as a matter of individual responsibility rather than collective guilt. It is incumbent upon newspapers to uphold consistency and fairness, avoiding language that isolates or stigmatizes communities unless such information is truly essential to understanding the story at hand.

As readers, we depend upon the media to enlighten us impartially. When a publication chooses to accentuate the ethnicity of suspects in certain cases yet disregards it in others, it betrays that fundamental trust.

The Tribune - and all news outlets - should reconsider these editorial choices and pledge themselves to reporting that honors all communities with equal respect. Only then can we earnestly aspire to a media landscape grounded in true fairness and accuracy.

- George Reveliotis, president, ENOSIS

My mother the superhero

I grew up in a house that doubled as my father's business. He was an accountant, and my mother was his secretary. Dinner conversations sounded more like office meetings than family meals, filled with words like payroll, quarterly reports, invoices and W-2s. My brother and I had no idea what any of it meant.

It seemed my parents were always working. Even weekends were rarely free. My mother would leave her desk only to cook, clean the house and iron clothes, then return to work. My father called her his "Girl Friday." She didn't prepare complicated tax returns, but she handled countless tasks for the business and did them exceptionally well.

When I was about 10 years old, my father got into legal trouble unrelated to his accounting practice. He was convicted of mail fraud and sentenced to a year in federal prison. Overnight, my mother was left to hold the business and the family together.

My father's clients were loyal and had become close friends over the years, but my mother knew little about the technical side of accounting or tax preparation. It seemed impossible that the business could survive.

Then I watched my mother transform almost overnight from a secretary into a full-fledged accountant. My uncle, who was also an accountant, stepped in to help with the workload, but my mother learned quickly. Before long, she was meeting with clients, preparing tax returns and running the office with a confidence that surprised everyone.

She never complained. Not once.

Every Saturday, we drove two hours each way to visit my father in prison. My mother always brought him several cartons of cigarettes. In prison, cigarettes were as good as currency.

I will never forget that year. My mother became my hero. She held the business together and did not lose a single client. When my father finally came home, he returned to a business that was not broken, but thriving - thanks entirely to her strength, loyalty and resolve.

My father never forgot it either.

- Scott T. Thompson, Bloomington, Indiana

The myths told to women

Mother's Day is here and seems as good a time as any for women to finally level with one another. Following are a few of the myths that women continue to perpetuate.

1. You can have it all.

Telling girls that they can "have it all," and the slightly less false, "You can have it all, just not at the same time," is misleading at best. Young women who are considering raising a family should know that parenting is more demanding - both physically and emotionally - than they might think. Prepare accordingly in selecting a life partner and career.

2. Childbirth is hard.

This is like saying, "Living through a pandemic is inconvenient." I never worried about the physical pain of childbirth when I was pregnant because I blithely assured myself that I would receive an epidural and thus experience no pain or at worst a bit of discomfort during the blessed event. It turned out, for reasons that still aren't entirely clear to me, that I went for hours - or maybe it just seemed like hours - before I received said epidural. If you're pregnant, expect to be in excruciating pain at some point during childbirth. Full stop.

3. Menopause is a relief.

Since getting my period at the age of 13, there was always a part of me that looked forward to "the change" and associated release from the hassles of menstruation. These hassles have now been replaced with daily unbidden hot flashes and night sweats, equally, if not more, uncomfortable and inconvenient than having my period each month.

Why are women not honest with each other about these and other facts? Do we lie or at best obfuscate to avoid being the bearers of bad news? Must every generation fend for itself? Is it some kind of biological amnesia necessary to propagate the species?

I hope someday to have a granddaughter of my own to pass along these and other pearls of wisdom (e.g., nearly anyone can be attractive; some just have to work at it more than others). If not, this piece will have to suffice.

Happy Mother's Day!

- Stacy J. Seiden, Northbrook

Note to readers

In honor of our "Chicago 2050" op-ed series, we'd like to hear from you about your hopes for what Chicago will be like in 25 years. (Sincere thoughts only.) Send a letter of no more than 400 words to letters@chicagotribune.com Be sure to include your full name and your city/town.

value.Title: Reactivate Partners with Tribal Energy Alternative to Support Workforce Training for Tribal Members in 5 States
value.Overview: PR Newswire, 604words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHM-0DD3-S4G1-44VK-00000-00Reactivate Partners with Tribal Energy Alternative to Support Workforce Training for Tribal Members in 5 States2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:43:30ZLexisNexisReactivate Partners with Tribal Energy Alternative to Support Workforce Training for Tribal Members in 5 StatesCHICAGO, May 7, 2026

PR NewswireCHICAGO, May 7, 2026 /PRNewswire/ --Reactivateis proud to announce a new partnership withTribal Energy Alternatives (TEA)to support clean energy workforce training for members in Tribal Nations. Funded through Reactivate's Renewable Energy Equity Foundation (REEF), this partnership will train up to 50 Tribal members through a solar curriculum, providing hands-on installation experience, industry certifications, and the buildout of a dedicated solar lab to meet the rising demand for a reliable, locally supported power grid.

With an aging energy grid and the need for more electricity supply due to increased demands, the industry faces a critical shortage of trained workers. The partnership focuses on the Tribal Workforce Development project led by TEA. Through grant funding from REEF, the program will facilitate renewable energy training across 5 states including Colorado, New Mexico, Oklahoma, South Dakota, and Washington—as well as the buildout of a dedicated solar lab."Recognizing the vital role developers play in the clean energy workforce, Reactivate is excited to partner with TEA and tribal communities to support a program that produces tangible outcomes and supports Tribal members," said Utopia Hill, CEO of Reactivate.Program Highlights & ImpactThis initiative is designed to move beyond traditional classroom instruction by providing holistic support for participants:Comprehensive Curriculum: The program aims to graduate up to 50 trainees.Certifications: Trainees will receive hands-on solar installation experience, along with OSHA 10 and CPR certifications.Financial & Career Support: To ensure long-term career success, the funding supports trainee stipends, resume building, and job placement assistance.Technical Infrastructure: Funding will directly support the development of a solar lab for ongoing technical education."The REEF Reactivate partnership is about more than funding, it's about unlocking opportunity," said Tanksi Clairmont, Co-Executive Director of TEA. "By investing in the place-based tribal workforce and training model at TEA, we're supporting tribal communities across five states with the skills, resources, and pathways needed to lead in the clean energy economy for years to come."About ReactivateReactivate, an Invenergy company, is a mission-driven organization that develops, owns, and operates renewable energy solutions that are designed to improve quality of life for communities, with a focus on meaningful benefits for working-class people across the country. Focus areas include community solar, commercial & industrial solar, small utility-scale solar, energy storage and EV charging projects.By delivering economic development and energy resiliency with renewable energy solutions, Reactivate provides energy cost savings, job opportunities, workforce training and opportunities for businesses, while creating positive social and environmental impact. Reactivate was founded by Invenergy and Lafayette Square.‍About Tribal Energy Alternative (TEA)Tribal Energy Alternativesworks in partnership with Tribal communities to advance energy sovereignty and accelerate the transition to clean energy across Indian Country. Through programs like the Tribal Solar Accelerator Fund, TEA provides funding, technical assistance, and strategic support to help Tribes achieve energy self-determination and climate resilience.
